More importantly I’m out of position against the opener and his range contains many hands that already beat me (top pair, overcards, pinned pairs) or will continue pressure on later streets. A raise/bluff here is thin on a dry board and would commit chips in a difficult spot. With a healthy 7.5k stack and plenty of hands left in the tournament, folding preserves chips and sticks to my plan.

3♦8♣ is a marginal, unsuited hand — playable in position but not worth opening wide against two competent, deep opponents who have shown aggression. The call is cheap (100 into a 150 pot) and preserves position postflop, letting me see a flop and proceed only with a meaningful draw or pair. If either opponent shows significant strength (a 3‑bet or large continuation), I’ll fold — I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with this holding. Therefore I will just call and use position to realize equity or look for a good spot to bluff on later streets.

I am in the Small Blind holding Ten of Hearts and Deuce of Clubs, which is a very weak hand. The Button (Minni) has limped into the pot. While I only have 50 more to call to see a flop, T2o has extremely poor playability out of position, especially in a potential three-way pot if the Big Blind checks. To maintain a disciplined strategy and avoid marginal situations with trash hands, I will fold.

Preflop in BB, facing a button limp after SB fold. Hand 4s6d is speculative trash offsuit with poor playability. Pot odds excellent (already invested BB, pot 250). Checking realizes equity for cheap multiway potential, maintains positional advantage postflop vs Minni who limped (ranges wide, sometimes weak). Raising would be bluffy with poor postflop prospects and risks building pot OOP with marginal hand. Goal to maximize stack long-term favors seeing flops cheaply in position.

On the flop of 4d Js Tc, holding 4s6d for bottom pair with no kicker and no straight or flush draw. OOP vs Minni's wide button limp range, which includes many hands that miss (overcards, weak pairs, draws) but also some that hit J/T or have overpairs. Pot is 250, SPR ~33 with my stack. Betting OOP risks getting raised by better or called by worse that improves; thin value unlikely to get called by worse often enough. Checking keeps pot small, induces bluffs/checks from missed range, allows check-raise if she bets weak, or check-call small bet for pot control. Fits patient tournament strategy, avoiding spew with marginal holdings early in multi-hand tourney.

I have 3♦8♣ on a 4♦ J♠ T♣ flop — no pair, no meaningful draw (only six low outs: three 3s and three 8s). Pot is small (250) and there is currently no bet to call. I'm on the button in position with one opponent (Elon) who checked the flop; he has shown aggression earlier but checked here, signaling either a miss or a slowplay. With essentially zero showdown value and only ~24% to improve by river, I don't want to bloat this pot or bluff into a competent opponent with little fold equity. Best line is to check and realize equity cheaply; if Elon fires a sizable bet I will fold most of the time, only calling very small probes that give correct pot odds to chase my few outs. Checking preserves my stack for better spots and keeps the decision simple on the turn.

On the turn Ace of Clubs, the board is 4d Js Tc Ac, giving straight possibilities like KQ and hitting many Ace-high hands in Minni's wide button limp-call range (AJ, AT, Aces, etc.). My 4s6d is bottom pair no kicker, with minimal showdown value OOP and poor equity against her likely improved range. Pot is 250 with deep stacks (SPR ~33), so betting risks raising/folding to raises while getting little value from worse; thin value bets get called by better. Checking maintains pot control, induces bluffs from her missed draws/overcards, allows check-call to small bets for pot odds or check-fold larger, preserving stack in patient tournament play vs calling station tendencies observed in Minni's history (frequent calls preflop/flop). Fits GTO multiway/low SPR spot by realizing equity passively.

On the turn (4♦ J♠ T♣ A♣) with 3♦8♣ I have no pair and only six realistic outs (three 3s and three 8s) — ~13% to hit by the river. The pot is small (250) and Elon checked the flop and turn, so betting here would be a thin bluff with very limited fold equity against a competent opponent who can have Ax, Jx, Tx, KQ, or better. If I bet and get called or raised I'm usually in bad shape; better EV is to control the pot and realize equity. I will check and fold to significant river aggression, only calling very small probes that give the correct pot odds to chase my six outs.

River 6h pairs my 6d, upgrading to two pair (6s over 4s) on board 4d Js Tc Ac 6h. Minni's button limp-check-check-check range is very wide: weak Ax (top pair), J/T/9/8x pairs, busted draws (flush/straight missed), high cards. Strong hands like sets (JJ/TT/44/AA/66 unlikely from limp), two pair (J10/A4/J6 etc.) or KQ broadway possible but thin. OOP first to act with showdown value and thin value range, betting extracts from her calling station tendencies (history of loose calls like Hand 1,15,19,25). Pot 250, bet 2/3 pot (170) sizes to get called by single pairs/A-high while denying equity/representing strength vs bluffs. Checking risks free showdown to better or her bluff-betting; value bet maximizes EV in deep tournament spot.

River decision: I have 3♦8♣ on 4♦ J♠ T♣ A♣ 6♥ — no pair and zero showdown value. Pot is 420 and Elon has bet 170; calling 170 to win a 760 pot after my call requires Elon to be bluffing >170/760 ≈ 22.4% of the time to break even. Given his line (checking flop and turn then a small river probe) he can easily have thin value hands here (Ax, Jx, Tx, small pairs, or a slow-played two‑pair) as often as he is bluffing. I have no blockers and no realistic fold equity, so a call is a marginal bluff-catcher and a raise would be pure theatrics with almost no fold equity and large downside. Preserving a 7,400 stack and waiting for a better spot is higher EV than calling here. Fold.

I am on the button with Q5o in a 3-handed game. While I have a significant chip lead, Q5o is a weak hand that doesn't play well post-flop if called, and both opponents have shown a willingness to defend their blinds. Given the stack sizes, I don't need to force the action with sub-marginal hands. Folding is the disciplined play here to wait for better spots to utilize my stack pressure.

Preflop in SB heads-up vs BB after Button fold. Pocket 3s is a strong enough hand to raise for value, thin value, and fold equity. Minni has defended blinds frequently in history and calls stations in spots, but our stacks are deep (84BB effective). Raising builds the pot for set value and takes initiative. Standard sizing 3x BB to 300 total. Min raise is 200, but 300 pressures better.

Facing a 300 open from the small blind with Q♠6♠ in the big blind. Pot is 400 and I need to call 200 more (total 300). Q6s is a speculative, playable hand with flush/backdoor possibilities and decent equity against a wide SB opening range — Elon has shown frequent preflop aggression and continuation betting, so I expect a fairly wide range. Calling gives me ~33% pot odds to see a flop and preserves stack depth (stacks are deep ~73bb). A min 3-bet to 500 is too small to fold out many hands and I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand. Plan: see the flop and continue only with a queen, spade draw, or favorable texture; otherwise concede to heavy aggression.

Pocket 3s is a strong underpair on a dry K-J-4 rainbow flop that favors my preflop raising range. The board misses most of Minni's calling range (Axo, broadway, suited connectors), which often contains overcards, weak broadway, or pocket pairs below J. As the preflop aggressor OOP, I should c-bet for thin value against Ax-high and weak pairs, protection against overcards that can improve, and fold equity. Minni has shown station tendencies in history but folds to continued aggression when missed. Half-pot sizing (300 into 600) balances value, fold equity, and keeps pot manageable deepstacked (71BB effective). If called, I can evaluate turn dynamics; if raised, I have fold equity still.

Elon c-bet 300 into a 900 pot; calling 300 commits me 300 to win 1200 (≈25% pot odds). I hold Q♠6♠ with no pair and no spade on the flop (J♦ 4♥ K♣). My realistic equity to improve to a competitive hand by showdown is well under the ~25% needed: ~12–13% to hit a queen by the river plus a small (~5%) chance of a backdoor flush (needs both turn and river spades). Elon is a frequent preflop raiser and continuation better — his range here will include Kx, Jx, Ax and many broadways that currently dominate me or have decent equity. Raising as a bluff OOP against an aggressive, fairly sticky opponent and deep stacks is risky and likely to get me into a bloated pot with poor equity. Following my preflop plan to only continue with a queen, a visible spade draw, or a clearly favorable texture, I should fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

AcQd is a premium hand on the button in a 3-handed game. Excellent position to play post-flop, strong equity against calling ranges from blinds. Standard play is to raise for value and initiative, building the pot while denying equity to weaker hands. Opponents have shown willingness to call raises from history, but our position allows control. Raising to 3x the BB (300 total) is balanced sizing early in the tournament with deep stacks (88BB effective vs SB). Avoid limping or flatting as it invites multiway pots OOP.

Facing a standard 3bb open to 300 from the button with K♥T♣ in the small blind, I prefer to defend rather than fold or thinly 3‑bet. KTo has good playability in a 3‑handed pot and calling keeps the pot manageable while preserving stack depth and postflop options. A raise to 500 is a marginal effort with this hand that can get me isolated or 3‑bet; folding is too tight here. I’ll call to see a flop and proceed cautiously — look to continue when I hit (K/T or strong draws) and fold or give up to heavy aggression on dry misses.

I am in the Big Blind with 6s 4s. Elon raised to 300 from the Button, and Minni called from the Small Blind. I need to put in 200 more to call into a pot of 700. This gives me pot odds of 4.5:1. Suited gappers like 64s play well in multi-way pots when the price is this attractive. Given my significant chip lead and the excellent odds, calling to see a flop is the most profitable play.

I hold K♥T♣ on Jh Ts 3h — I’ve hit second pair (tens) with a strong K kicker. This is a three‑way pot (BTN preflop raiser + BB caller) and I’m first to act; checking hands the initiative back to Elon and gives free cards to many draws/Jx hands. I’ll make a modest value/protection c‑bet ~1/3 pot (300) to get value from worse tens and weaker Jx, and to charge straight/heart draw equity. I’m not committed — if Elon puts in substantial raise I’ll fold and preserve my stack; if called I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously.

I am in the Big Blind with 6s 4s on a flop of Jh-Ts-3h. Minni has led out for 300 into a 900 pot. I have no pair and no real draw on this board. While the pot odds are 5:1 (300 to win 1500), I have almost zero equity against the ranges of Minni (who led) and Elon (the pre-flop raiser still to act). There is no reason to continue with this hand.

Preflop raise with AQo from button is standard and gives us a strong perceived range. Flop Jh Ts 3h is drawy with two hearts and some straight gutshots possible (e.g., KQ, Q9, 89). Minni's donk bet of 300 into a ~900 pot from SB after calling pre is polarized: either value (top pair like JJ/TJ/J3, sets, or strong draws like nut flush draw or combo draws) or a bluff/draw trying to take it away OOP. Flash's fold to the bet suggests weakness. We have position, ace-high showdown value, blockers to strong Ax hands (Ac blocks Ah nut flush draw and strong pairs), and great playability postflop. Direct equity vs her value range (e.g., JJ: ~32%, flush draws: 40%+) is decent, but we have strong fold equity on her bluffs/draws. Raising exploits her likely wide range, denies equity to draws, builds pot when ahead, and takes initiative multiway history shows Minni calls stations but folds to aggression (e.g., Hand 33, 32). Sizing to 900 (3x her bet, ~25% pot after) balances value/bluff, with SPR low vs her 6500 stack. Call is playable but passive OOP disadvantage post-turn; fold too tight for our image/range.

Flop: Jh Ts 3h. I have K♥T♣ — second pair (tens) with a very good K‑kicker and the King of hearts which also blocks some heart semi‑bluffs. I c‑bet 300 to get value/protect; Elon raises to 900. Facing a single raise in a 3‑handed pot, folding is too tight (hand has decent showdown value) and re‑raising as a bluff is high risk out of position against a range that contains many Jx, sets or strong pairs. Calling keeps the pot under control, gets the correct pot odds (calling 600 into ~2100 requires ~22% equity), and lets me re‑evaluate on the turn — if I improve (another T or a favorable card) I can extract value; if Elon continues heavy aggression on a dry turn I can fold and preserve my stack. Therefore I call the raise and see the turn.

Board is J♥ T♠ 3♥ 7♥; I hold K♥ T♣ — second pair (tens) with a very strong K kicker plus a king‑high heart draw. With three hearts on board I have 4 hearts including my K, giving ~9 heart outs plus the remaining non‑heart ten (≈10 unique outs, ~21–22% to improve on the river) and I already have some showdown value against bluffs and weaker pairs. Elon’s flop raise polarized his range toward top pair (Jx), sets or strong made hands plus some semi‑bluffs; I’m out of position so I don’t want to inflate the pot with a hand that is often behind. I’ll check to control the pot, induce bluffs, and see how Elon reacts. If he bets a small/moderate amount I’ll call to realize my draw and showdown equity; I’ll fold to very large overbets that look strongly value‑heavy.

Turn board brings the 7h, adding a third heart to the J♥ T♠ 3♥ flop, completing flush draws and increasing straight draw possibilities (e.g., KQ, 98o/suited). Minni's line—SB limp-call pre, flop donk into multiway pot, then hero-raise call for 600 into 2100+ pot (full stack commitment possible)—is strong: favors made hands like top pair (AJ, KJ, QT, JTs), two-pair/combo (JT, J3 unlikely), sets (JJ/TT/33), or strong draws (nut flush draw like AhXh, combo straight-flush draws). Her turn check after paying off flop raise suggests either trapping a monster, weak made hand (middle pair/draw no longer good), or completed flush/draw wary of further action. We hold AcQd: ace-high with backdoor broadway/fd blockers (Ac blocks Ah/nut flush draw Ax, Qd blocks KQo broadway), but no pair/draw, pure showdown value. Preflop raiser + flop raiser image reps strong (AJ+/sets/draws), but turn texture hits villain range hard, capping our value and exposing bluffs to calls. Pot 2700 vs Minni's 5900 (effective SPR ~2.2); betting risks commitment OOP vs station (history: calls big pots light, e.g., Hands 1,13,15,25,28) without fold equity (her check invites bluffs but calls value), while thin value bets get snapped by better aces/pairs/flushes. Checking retains max equity (35-45% vs her range: beats bluffs/weak pairs, lives vs draws), controls pot, induces bluffs river if checked through, exploits position in 3-handed dynamic where Minni folds to river aggression often (Hands 11,32). Denies her free card on scary river cards; if monster, she might bet, allowing fold; if weak, realizes equity cheap. Superior to betting (low EV thin value/bluff into sticky range) or folding (never). Plan: check turn, eval river based on action/board.

I have TcKh on J♥ T♠ 3♥ 7♥ 3♣ — that gives me two pair (Tens and Threes) but it's a vulnerable hand. Elon’s line (flop raise over my c‑bet, then turn check and river check) is heavily weighted toward Jx and strong made hands that beat my two pair, with some sets/overpairs mixed in. Betting the river is unattractive: worse hands will often fold and better hands (Jx, sets/full houses) will call, so a value bet will be called by hands that beat me and fold the hands I can extract thin value from. With stacks of 5900/7600 and a 2700 pot, the safest +EV line is to avoid bloating the pot and take the showdown — check and see his cards.

River 3c pairs the deuce (now trips possible with 33, J3, T3 unlikely in range), completes some straight draws (e.g., 89, 45 unlikely), and adds little new draws but bricks most flush misses. Minni's full line (SB call pre, flop donk bet into 3-way pot ~900, call 600 raise to ~2400 pot, check turn after paying off, check river) remains capped at strong made hands: flushes (AhXh, KhJh, etc.), two-pair (JT, J3 rare, T7h?), sets/trips (JJ/TT/33/J3h), or slowplayed monsters. Her check after check-call flop raise suggests not trapping nuts (would bet turn/river for value vs my range), more likely medium strength wary of further action or weak value/draw that hit or missed. We hold AcQd: pure ace-high showdown value, blocks nut flush (Ah), broadway (KQ), strong Ax pairs; beats her bluffs, missed draws (e.g., combo straight-flush draws like 89s, KQs), naked Aces, weaker pairs (77, 33? unlikely). Equity ~40-50% vs her capped range (strong but not uncallable). Pot 2700, SPR ~2.2 vs her 5900; betting risks (thin value called by better Ax/flushes/pairs, bluffs called by her station tendencies per history: Hands 1,5,13,15,25,28 calls big pots light OOP); no fold equity post her river check. Checking maximizes EV: realizes full equity/showdown value uncontested (beats ~35% her range: bluffs/weak Ax/pairs), induces her bluffs if she has them (unlikely but free), controls pot size in tournament (115 hands left, deep effective stacks), exploits position/3-handed dynamic where check-backs win frequently (opponents passive river OOP). Superior to betting (negative EV thin value/bluff) or folding (surrendering equity). History: Minni checks back medium strength often (Hands 1,13,25,28,33), we take it down.

7♠3♥ is a very weak, unsuited hand with almost no post‑flop playability. Pot is 150 and it costs 100 to see the flop (you’d need ~40% equity just to break even on the call), which 7‑3 offsuit doesn’t have against one random hand and is even worse multiway. Raising to 200 is a poor bluff here — Flash (SB) will frequently defend and Elon (BB) has shown aggression in prior hands, so I’d risk a bloated pot or a 3‑bet out of position. With ~86 BB I can wait for a much better spot. Fold and preserve the stack.

I am in the Small Blind with Kd 6s. Since the Button has folded, it is now a head-up situation between me and Elon (the Big Blind). King-high is relatively strong in a blind-vs-blind battle. While I could limp (call the extra 50), raising is generally the better play to seize the initiative and potentially win the pot immediately. Elon has shown a lot of pre-flop aggression in previous hands, so putting him on the defensive is a sound strategy. A standard raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is appropriate here.

75o is a very weak hand with poor playability out of position. Facing a standard 3x raise from the SB after button folds, this is a clear fold. SB may be wide, but my hand has low equity against any reasonable range and no blockers. Pot odds are decent at 2:1 but postflop OOP realization is poor. Preserve chips for better spots.

I am on the button in a three-handed game with Ten-Seven offsuit. While I have a dominant chip lead and could potentially bully the shorter stacks of Elon and Minni, T7o is a marginal hand even in a three-max environment. Since I have been quite active in previous hands, I will take this opportunity to fold and maintain a disciplined image, rather than over-committing with a weak starting hand.

Preflop in SB with 2d7s, one of the worst hands in poker, offsuit, unconnected, low cards. Facing BB only after BTN fold. Deep stacks, but completing OOP would be spewy as I have no postflop playability. Raising to steal possible but Minni defends/ calls frequently based on history. Standard and optimal is to fold and wait for better spots to maximize stack long-term.

3-handed on the button with K8o in an unraised pot (both blinds limped in, pot 150, to call 100). This is a strong spot with position. K8o is a profitable raising hand heads-up or multiway vs blinds 3-handed due to king-high equity, straight potential (78), and good postflop playability. Opponents have shown tendencies to call wide (e.g., Minni calls a lot, Flash aggressive but folds to pressure sometimes). Raising isolates, takes initiative, and builds pot with position. Standard size around 3x BB. Raise to 300 total. Folding too tight, limping weak from button.

I am in the small blind with T♥6♠ facing a button open to 300 (pot 450, 250 to call). T6o is a weak, poorly situated hand — dominated by a large portion of the button's opening range and with poor playability out of position against both the opener and a deep-stacked big blind who can call or 3-bet. Calling 250 into a 450 pot requires ~250/(450+250)=35.7% equity to break even; T6o's equity vs a realistic 3-handed BTN range is well below that and postflop OOP decisions will be difficult. With deep stacks I can wait for better spots or a hand with fold equity/blockers. Fold and preserve the stack for more favourable opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind with 6h 3c, which is a very weak hand. Elon raised to 3x from the Button. Although I have a significant chip lead and the pot odds are roughly 2.25 to 1, this hand is too weak to defend profitably, especially out of position. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I have pocket deuces (2s 2d) on the button with ~86bb. I’ll open-raise to 300 (≈3x the big blind). Rationale: button + pocket pair = take initiative and build fold equity against the blinds while keeping pot size manageable. Raising isolates opponents and gives better postflop position if called; calling 100 to set-mine is reasonable, but a standard 3x open often wins the pot outright or pays off when a set hits. Plan if action continues: fold to a large, polarized 3-bet; consider calling a small 3-bet only when pot odds/implied odds justify set-mining; if called, play for sets and control the pot on dangerous boards.

I am in the Small Blind with T8s (Ten of Clubs, Eight of Clubs). Minni (the Button) has raised to 300, and the Big Blind (Elon) is yet to act. Both opponents have around 75-83 Big Blinds. T8s is a solid semi-connected suited hand that plays well in multi-way pots and can flop significant equity. While I could 3-bet to try and take the pot immediately, calling is a standard and safe play here to see a flop given the pot odds (250 to call into 450). This allows me to keep the pot manageable while retaining the ability to realize my hand's equity.

74o is a very weak hand with no connectivity, suitedness, or playability. Facing a button raise from Minni (who is aggressive but positionally wide) and a small blind call from deep-stacked Flash, the multiway pot post-flop would be tough with this hand from BB position. Pot odds of calling 200 into 700 (pot will be 900) are about 4:1, but equity against their ranges is too low (~20-25% multiway) to justify. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots in tournament with 7500 effective.

On this flop of Js-9h-3d, I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Queen to complete a 8-9-T-J-Q straight). Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Minni), the standard play is to check to the raiser. Donk-betting here doesn't serve much purpose as the board favors the button's range, which includes all overpairs and strong Jacks. I will check and see how Minni proceeds; if she bets, I will have to evaluate the price to continue with my gutshot.

I raised preflop and have position on the SB. Board (J♠ 9♥ 3♦) is dry and rainbow — a good spot to take the initiative with a small continuation bet. My pocket deuces have little showdown value but benefit from folding out overcards and unpaired hands while keeping the pot controlled if called. I’ll bet ~1/3 pot (250) to get value/folds without bloating the pot; if I’m raised big I’ll fold (call only very small, priced-in raises when set-mining odds/implied justify). If called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously — look to check back blanks and ipush when I improve to a set.

I have a gutshot straight draw with my Ten-Eight on a Jack-Nine-Three board, needing a Queen to complete a straight. Minni has made a small continuation bet of 250 into a 700 pot (roughly 35% of the pot). I am getting pot odds of nearly 4:1, which is more than enough to justify calling with a gutshot (which has about an 8.5% chance of hitting on the turn, but significant implied odds if I do hit). I will call here to see if I can improve on the turn.

On the turn, the King of Diamonds appears, which doesn't improve my Ten-high but maintains my gutshot straight draw (needing a Queen). Since I am out of position and Minni was the pre-flop and flop aggressor, the most prudent play is to check. This allows me to see how she reacts to the King, potentially taking a free card if she checks behind or evaluating the pot odds if she decides to bet.

Check. I have bottom pair (22) with one diamond and the turn K♦ is a high card that helps a lot of hands in Flash’s calling range (Kx, Jx, 9x, pocket pairs). Flash’s line (call flop, check turn) shows weakness but also includes many hands that currently beat me — betting here would be a bluff that’s likely to get called by better hands and folded to only some A/Q-highs. With ~80bb effective and a pot of 1,200 I want pot control and to preserve fold equity for the river (or hope to see a deuce for a set). Plan: check and re-evaluate on the river — call small river bets if they’re bluff-sized and fold to sizable pressure unless I improve.

I have Ten-high with no showdown value. The board is 9h-Js-3d-Kd-4d. Minni bet the flop but checked the turn, which usually indicates a marginal made hand (like a 9 or a weak Jack) or a complete air hand that gave up. While the King on the turn was a scare card that she checked, my own range contains some Kings and Jacks. However, looking at Minni's history in previous hands (Hand 15, Hand 19, Hand 25, Hand 27), she has a strong tendency to check-call throughout multiple streets with middle-market hands. Bluffing into a 'calling station' profile is generally a negative-EV play. Since I cannot win at showdown, and a bluff is unlikely to succeed against her tendencies, I will check and concede the pot.

Check. I have bottom pair (2♠2♦) on J♠ 9♥ 3♦ K♦ 4♦ and the pot is 1,200. Flash’s line (call flop, check turn, check river) is passive and contains many hands that currently beat me (Kx, Jx, 9x, medium pairs) and are unlikely to fold to a river bluff. Betting would be a thin bluff that will mostly be called by better or fold only to obvious bluffs, so I choose pot control and go to showdown. If Flash bets a small, obvious block/bluff-sized bet I’ll consider a call; I’ll fold to sizable value pressure.

I am on the button with Deuce of Hearts and Nine of Spades (92o), which is a very weak hand. Even in a three-handed game, 92o is generally outside of a profitable opening range. With Elon having a relatively short stack of about 74 big blinds, there is a risk of a shove over my steal attempt. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Preflop in SB vs BB after BTN fold. Holding 3h5h, suited connectors with straight and flush potential. Excellent pot odds: calling 50 more into 150 pot (3:1). Postflop position advantage on SB. Minni defends BB wide based on history of frequent calls. Deep stacks (74BB effective). Folding too tight; raising risks playing OOP if she 3-bets. Best to realize equity cheaply by completing.

We’re in the big blind with Q♦T♥ and there’s been no raise (Elon just limped/called 50). This is a marginal, speculative hand — playable but not strong enough to open-raise against a potentially aggressive opponent out of position. The pot is tiny (200) relative to our stacks, so we can see a flop for free and keep the pot small. I’ll check to see the flop, evaluate board texture and Elon’s action, and proceed postflop (c-bet or fold/float) depending on how it develops.

Flop 4h9hKc gives me the nut flush draw (9h outs) plus open-ended straight draw (8 outs: 2x or 6x, minimal overlap), roughly 32-35% equity against Minni's wide BB defending range. Board is draw-heavy with two hearts and Kc overcard, hitting few strong made hands but many weak Kx, Ax pairs, and bluffs. OOP but deep effective stacks (~74BB). Semi-bluff bet to apply pressure: charges draws worse than mine (non-nut flush draws, gutshots), gets value from floats/pairs, denies equity to her checking range. Check risks giving free card to overcards/draws or facing check-raise. Bet sizing ~60% pot (125 into 200) balances value/thin and fold equity without overcommitting. History shows Minni calls loosely but folds to aggression when behind (e.g., Hand 11, 16). Maximizes EV by realizing equity aggressively while building pot OOP.

We’re facing a small c-bet (125) on Kc‑9h‑4h after Elon limped/called preflop. My hand (Q♦T♥) has no pair but has a one‑card gutshot to a J (turn or river) and some showdown/float value against a wide limp/c-bet range. The bet is small relative to the pot: calling 125 into a 325 pot (pot becomes 450) requires ~27.8% equity. Against a limp/c-bet range I expect my hand’s combined equity (the chance to catch a J or win at showdown vs bluffs/weak holdings) to be in the neighborhood where a call is justified, and the cost is small relative to my stack. Raising here would be overplaying a marginal holding and could commit me out of position against Kx or better. Plan: call to see the turn, fold to sizeable turn aggression if I miss, or extract/pressure if I hit a J, Q, or a favorable heart/blank turn and Elon shows weakness.

Turn 5d is a relatively blank card for most ranges: doesn't complete the flush, adds a low pair on board (possible two-pair or set for specific hands), but doesn't hit many straight draws in her BB calling range. My hand improved to bottom pair (5s with 5h topper) plus the nut flush draw (9 outs, ~18% equity standalone), giving ~25-30% equity vs her flop-calling range (weak Kx, 9x, mid pairs, missed draws). OOP as SB, continuing aggression builds the pot when ahead or semi-ahead, charges her draws (e.g., non-nut flush draws like KhQh, gutshots), extracts value from floats/weak pairs (she's shown stationy tendencies but vulnerable post-flop). Checking risks giving free cards to overcards/Kx or facing bets; pot control suboptimal with strong equity deep-stacked (~70BB eff). History (e.g., Hands 15,19,25) shows she calls loosely but we can rep strength after flop bet. Half-pot bet ~225 balances value/thin value/fold equity without bloating pot too much if raised. Maximizes EV by denying equity and getting called by worse.

Call 225. Board is 4h-9h-Kc-5d; I hold QdTh. Elon limped and has c-bet/have small turn bet — his range is wide (Kx, 9x, medium pairs, missed draws and some air). The pot is 675 and it costs 225 to call, so I need ≈25% equity. My immediate outs: 4 jacks (straight) and 3 queens + 3 tens to pair (10 naive outs → ~21.7% to hit by the river). Q/T pairs aren’t always clean versus Kx, but given his limp/CB line and the relatively small price to see the river, calling is the prudent, low-risk play. If I miss the river I’ll fold to significant aggression; if I hit a J (straight) I’ll extract value, and if I pair Q/T I’ll evaluate opponent’s river action for value-call or a small showdown bet.

River 4d pairs the 4h, bricking flush and improving straight draws minimally while pairing the board low. My 5h pairs the 5d for middle pair with poor 3h kicker (~top 25% of pairs possible). Board: 4h9hKc5d4d features possible Kx (beats me), 9x/K9 (beats), two-pair/set (beats), but I beat missed draws (non-nut flush chasers like QhJh, gutshots, Ax floats, weak pairs below 5). Her flop/turn calling range (loose BB defend) includes ~30-40% equity for me now: value owns bluffs/A-high/weak made hands she stations with. Double-barrel rep'd strength (flush draw/protection); OOP check induces her bluffs/value-bleeds, pots controls stack (~7k eff), showdowns worse per history (calls stations but passive, e.g., Hands 1,5,15). Betting thin-value risks raise-only-better (K9+, two-pair) or pot bloat OOP; check maximizes EV long-term in deep 3-handed dynamic.

Elon limped pre, c‑bet small on the K‑9‑4 flop and barreled another small turn bet. I called both with Q♢T♡ for pot odds and to exploit his wide limp/c‑bet range, but on the river I’ve missed completely — Q‑high with zero showdown value against most value hands. He checks the river, showing weakness. Checking back hands like mine just loses at showdown too often; a small, value‑denying bluff can win the pot now. I’ll make a modest probe of ~1/3 pot (300 into 900) to fold out missed Kx/9x/overcards and weak floats while keeping risk limited. If he calls I assume I’m usually beat and will let it go; if he raises I’ll fold and preserve a healthy stack.

Facing a 300 bet into 1200 pot (~4:1 odds, need ~20% equity) on paired board 4h9hKc5d4d with middle pair 5s and 3h kicker from SB after double-barreling flop/turn as semi-bluff/value with strong draw that missed. Minni's BB limp-call range vs my aggression: wide (weak Kx, 9x, mid-pairs like 77-TT, Ax floats, missed draws pre-turn). Her flop/turn calls cap her range somewhat (unlikely sets/two-pair given board texture and sizing), but she stations thinly per history (e.g., Hands 1,5,15,25,39 where she calls bets loosely OOP). River pairs low 4s, bricking flush/straight draws, improving some two-pair/set hands but favoring her bluffing missed draws (non-nut hearts like QhJh, KhTh), floats, or thin value with weak K/9. My equity ~35-45% vs her polarized value/bluff range (beats bluffs/A-high/weak pairs JJ-QQ? QQ<KK but beats mine? No, QQ pairs Q>5), loses to Kx/9x/two-pair but pot odds crush it. Deep effective stacks (~70BB), calling commits <5% stack, realizes equity/showdown value OOP, induces her future thin bets. Raising risks vs Kx/two-pair (fold equity low post-OOP check), folding too tight given price/history (she value-bets thin or bluffs weakly). Call maximizes EV long-term in 3-handed deep dynamic.

3-handed on the button with 7d9h offsuit. Pot 150 with 50 dead from SB Minni fold, facing BB Flash's 100. Deep stacks (82BB effective vs Flash's 231BB). 79o is within button opening range vs BB in 3-handed play: good position, playability for straights/flushes/draws, ~38-40% equity vs typical BB defending range. Flash has been aggressive but calls stations in spots; history shows I successfully raise button wide (hands 1,3,5,16,20, etc.). Standard raise to 3x BB (300 total) to steal dead money + BB, apply pressure postflop with position. Limping weak, folding too tight for tournament stack accumulation.

I’m in the small blind with 5♦7♣ — a weak, unsuited, low-connector with poor postflop playability out of position. Elon (button) opened to 300; I’d need to call 250 more. Facing a button open in 3‑handed with the big blind still to act, calling to play OOP is marginal and often dominated. A 3‑bet with this holding is reckless and unnecessarily commits chips. Pot odds (250 into ~700) don’t justify calling versus a reasonable open range, and Elon has shown frequent aggression postflop. I’ll preserve my 84bb stack and fold to wait for a better spot.

Ten-Nine offsuit (T9o) is a solid hand to defend the Big Blind with, especially when getting 2.25:1 odds on a call against a standard 3x Button raise. While I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, the hand's playability and current stack depth justify seeing a flop.

I have middle pair with a Ten on an Ace-high board. As the big blind who called a pre-flop raise, checking to the aggressor is the standard play with this category of hand to see how they respond to the board. Middle pair has decent showdown value but is not strong enough to lead out into the pre-flop raiser's range, which contains many Aces.

Preflop button raise with 79o took initiative, stole SB dead money, got called by BB Flash. Flop AhTs5h: Ace-high board with heart flush draw, possible straight draws (JQ, 67, 89). My wide button range hits this hard (AT/A5/Ax, Tx, hearts, suited connectors like 87/67/J9 for draws). 79h has backdoor heart flush draw and gutshot-ish if runner-runner, but primarily bluff representing value. Flash checks BB (weak/trap/slowplay?), standard to c-bet ~2/3 pot (400 into 650) for thin value from weaker Aces/pairs/draws and fold equity vs air/floats. Position allows profitable bet/call/raise dynamic. History: Flash calls light but folds to aggression postflop (hands 3,5,16,23); I've successfully c-bet similar spots often. Check risks giving free card to his equity (OESD/flush draws), conceding pot OOP. Deep stacks favor continuation.

I have middle pair (Tens) with a Nine kicker on an Ace-high board. This is a classic 'marginal made hand' situation where I have enough value to call a single continuation bet from the pre-flop aggressor, but not enough to raise. The board has a flush draw and some straight possibilities (KQ, KJ), so Elon could be betting with those or simply C-betting his entire button-opening range. I am getting 2.6:1 odds on a call, which is sufficient to see a turn and evaluate the action further.

I have third pair (Tens) on a board that now contains both an Ace and a King. The King is a significant scare card that connects well with the Button's opening range (Kx, QJ). My hand has some showdown value but is not strong enough to bet for value or to turn into a bluff. Checking is the standard play here to control the pot size and see if the opponent checks behind or bets, which will allow me to re-evaluate the strength of my hand.

Turn board Kd brings a new high card, potentially improving Flash's BB defending range (KQ, AK, KTs, etc.) while completing some straight draws (QJ) but not directly helping my 7d9h air. Board remains draw-heavy with live heart flush draw. Flash checks again OOP after calling flop c-bet, which could be weak pairs/draws/traps, but history shows he calls stations and slows down vs aggression (e.g., Hands 3,5,8,16,23). Pot 1450, my stack 7550 (~75BB effective vs his 225BB). As preflop raiser/c-bettor, my range polarized: strong value (ATs, sets, two-pair) or bluffs (suited connectors like 87s/67s for missed draws). 7d9h has no equity now (no pair/draw/gutshot), zero showdown value, but position allows controlling pot. Betting ~2/3 pot (900-1100) as bluff exploits his check (fold equity vs missed draws/weak Ax/Tx), thin value from weaker pairs, but risks stack-off vs improved Kx/traps deep-stacked; he may call light per history (Hands 2,5,13,18,27,40). Checking preserves stack, induces his bluffs/river bets (live draws/value), free river card harmless (minimal equity anyway), and balances range by not always firing air post-check. Tournament mid-stage (41/150), 3-handed dynamic favors patient play over high-variance bluffs without strong blockers/read. Check to realize equity positionally and avoid tough river spots.

On the river, the board pairs the Ten, giving me trips (Tens). My best five-card hand is T-T-T-A-K, using the Ace and King from the board. This is a very strong hand. Elon's turn check suggests he might have a marginal Ace or a King that was wary of the King or Ace already on the board. By betting here, I can extract value from those one-pair hands. While I could be beaten by a straight (QJ) or a full house (AT, KT, 55), my hand is too strong to check. A half-pot bet of 750 is a standard value-targeting size.

River board Ah Ts 5h Kd Tc pairs the tens, favoring Flash's BB calling range (Tx becomes two pair/trips, Kx top pair, AK etc. improve). Line: called preflop raise OOP, called flop c-bet on draw-heavy board (possible flush str8 draws, Ax/Tx), checked turn Kd (polarized weak/monster), now half-pot bet 750 into 1450 after pairing card. Bluff possible (missed hearts like KhQh/Jh9h, but Kd killed some; straight draws like 67/89 missed). However, value heavy: any 10 (AT/KT/QT/JT), AK/AQ/AJ, sets. My 7d9h pure air: no pair, draw, or blocker to his value. Pot odds ~2:1 (750 to win 2950, needs 28% equity), but 0% here. History shows Flash mixes aggression but bets river for value often (Hands 2,27); calling would be spew deep-stacked. Tournament stage 41/150, 3-handed, preserve 7550 stack (~75BB) for edges. Clear fold.

Holding 3♣7♠ — one of the weakest unsuited hands. There are still two opponents (SB Flash and BB Elon) and calling 100 into a 150 pot will very likely create a multiway pot where my hand has very poor equity and is frequently dominated by their defending ranges. Elon has shown frequent preflop aggression/defense in prior hands and Flash is deep and capable of calling/3‑betting, so a min‑raise to 200 is unlikely to pick up the blinds and would often put me in tough post‑flop spots against stronger ranges. My stack (8,450) is healthy enough to wait for a better spot; folding preserves chips and avoids a low‑EV confrontation. Fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 9c 8s, a decent suited connector-type hand in a short-handed (3-player) game. Minni has already folded on the button. I only need to call 50 more to see a flop, making the pot 200 total, or I can raise to put pressure on the Big Blind (Elon). Given the tournament stage and our stack sizes, calling is a low-risk way to realize equity, but raising is also viable to steal the blinds. However, with Elon being a somewhat active defender, I will complete the small blind to see a flop cheaply with a hand that has some connectivity.

Preflop in BB facing a limp from SB after BTN folds. Hand is 5d8c, offsuit gapped connectors, weak speculative hand with little showdown value. SB limp indicates wide range, likely to defend vs raises. Checking is best to see flop for free in position heads-up, realize equity if we hit straight/flush draws, or fold cheaply to postflop aggression. Raising risks playing out of position OOP with marginal hand vs deeper SB stack. Standard play is to check.

The flop comes K-K-K, which is a highly unusual board. My current hand is three Kings with a 9-8 kicker. Since I completed from the Small Blind and the Big Blind checked, both of our ranges are extremely wide. If either of us has the case King, it's Quads. If one of us has an Ace, they have a very strong full house (KKKAA). With 9-high, I have very little showdown value if Elon has any pair or an Ace. However, there's no reason to turn my hand into a bluff yet, as Elon might also have nothing. Checking is the most prudent play to see how he reacts and to see a cheap turn card.

The flop is K-K-K rainbow, an extremely dry board where my 5d8c has zero showdown value, no pair, no draws, and minimal bluff equity. Flash's SB limp preflop suggests a wide range, but their check behind indicates possible weakness, a trapped king, or confusion on this board. Betting air here is low EV as Flash can easily call or raise light with any king, ace, or even gutshots, forcing a fold and wasting chips OOP. Checking preserves my stack, keeps the pot small (200), and allows seeing a turn card for free. Post-turn, I can evaluate bluffing opportunities if Flash shows continued weakness or the board improves my playability, aligning with long-term stack maximization in a tournament with 108 hands left.

The board is K-K-K-J, which means everyone has at least three-of-a-kind with a Jack kicker. My best five-card hand is K-K-K-J-9. In a limped pot against the Big Blind, Elon could easily have an Ace, Queen, or Ten, all of which would result in a better five-card hand than mine. Betting here provides no value because better hands will call and worse hands will fold. I will check to keep the pot small and attempt to reach a cheap showdown.

Board is Kh Kd Kc Jc, providing trip kings to everyone with varying kickers. My 5d8c gives kings with 8-5 kickers, which is weak and likely behind Flash's SB limping range (wide, including Ax, broadways, pairs for full houses, suited connectors). Flash checked OOP flop and turn, showing possible weakness with inferior kickers or draws, but betting thin value or bluff risks getting called/raised by better hands while OOP on river if called. Pot is tiny (200) at 74BB deep, position allows free river card to realize any unlikely improvement or induce further weakness. Checking maximizes EV by controlling pot size, avoiding difficult spots, and preserving stack for better opportunities in tournament with 108 hands left.

The board has provided three Kings and a Jack, meaning our best hand is three-of-a-kind Kings with a Jack-Nine kicker (K-K-K-J-9). In a limped pot from the Small Blind against the Big Blind, Elon's range is extremely wide. He could easily hold an Ace, Queen, or Ten, all of which would result in a superior five-card hand. Since there is very little value in betting—as better hands will call and worse hands will simply fold—checking is the most logical choice to reach a cheap showdown and minimize risk in a small pot.

On this incredible board of triple kings (Kh Kd Kc) followed by Jc 5c, my 5d8c gives me kings full of fives (KKK55), an extremely strong hand that beats any bare king or lesser holdings. Flash limped from SB preflop (wide range including Ax, broadways, pairs, suited junk) and checked all four streets, indicating likely weakness: inferior kicker (e.g., Kx with x<5, unlikely), missed draw, or total air. No strong full houses like JJ or 55 are probable from SB limp range. Pot is tiny at 200 with deep stacks (74BB for me), so a value bet extracts from worse kings, top pairs (AJ+), or bluffs. Sizing 150 (3/4 pot) balances value and fold equity while protecting against unlikely superior full houses or quads. Checking risks giving Flash a free showdown or check-behind, missing value. Betting maximizes stack growth in tournament phase.

The board is K-K-K-J-5. My current best five-card hand is three Kings with a Jack-Nine kicker (K-K-K-J-9). In a limped pot between the Small Blind and Big Blind, ranges are extremely wide. However, any Ace, Queen, or Ten in Elon's hand would provide a better kicker and a superior five-card hand. Elon's bet of 150 into a 200 pot represents roughly 75% of the pot, which usually indicates either a strong kicker (like an Ace or Queen) value betting or a complete bluff. Given that many of the hands in a random Big Blind range contain an Ace, Queen, or Ten, a Nine-kicker is likely beaten or at best chopping. I will fold to preserve my stack and avoid calling off chips with a marginal kicker on this specific board.

I am on the button with T6s. While suited connectors or gappers are sometimes playable, T6 offsuit is generally too weak to open raise from the button, especially with relatively shallow stacks compared to the big blind and small blind's remaining chips. However, in this specific hand, I am facing a 50/100 blind structure and the pot is 150. I am the button. Looking at previous hand history, Minni and Elon have been active. Since I am in the button position, I could consider a raise to steal, but T6o is a very poor hand to defend if I get reraised. Folding is the most disciplined play here to preserve my stack as the chip leader.

Preflop in SB vs BB after BTN fold. Hand 8h3s is trash: low, offsuit, gapped, poor playability, low showdown value, minimal straight potential. Stack depths are deep (76BB), pot small (150). Standard SB completing range is ~40-50% of hands, but 83o is in bottom tier (~30th percentile equity vs random). EV of completing negative due to acting first postflop OOP with Minni likely to defend wide and value bet strong hands. Folding preserves stack for better spots in tournament with 150 hands remaining. No history suggests Minni folds BB often enough to exploit.

3-handed on the button with a deep effective stack. Pot is 150 with 100 to call, suggesting blinds are in and no prior raises (likely SB limped and BB checked, though pot size slightly unclear). A3o is a marginal hand but playable from the button in short-handed play. It blocks AA/AK, has ace-high potential, and position is excellent. Opponents from history defend wide and call frequently, but stealing blinds + antes (none here but dead money) is profitable. Standard aggressive open-raise sizing of 3x BB to 300 total to build pot or take it down pre-flop. Folding trash preserves stack for better spots; limping is poor. Raising maintains image as aggressive player seen in prior hands.

Elon opened to 300 from the button in a 3‑handed game. 6s9s is a playable suited connector against a wide button opening, but I'm out of position postflop and Flash (BB) still has to act. A 3‑bet here is thin — not enough hand or fold equity to comfortably isolate OOP — so I prefer to defend and see a flop. Calling keeps the pot controllable, takes advantage of implied odds and my flush/straight potential, and allows me to fold to heavy action if I miss. I will call the 250 additional chips (total committed 300 including my SB).

I am in the Big Blind holding As 6d. Elon raised to 300 from the Button, and Minni called from the Small Blind. I need to call 200 more chips to see a flop in a pot that will be 900 chips. This gives me pot odds of 4.5 to 1, which is very favorable. Given the strength of an Ace and the excellent price, I will call to close the pre-flop action and see a flop.

I have 9s6s on 4h‑Jh‑3h — I completely missed. The flop has three hearts, so many opponent hands (one or two hearts) either have a made flush or strong draw; I have no hearts and only backdoor spade equity (very small). I'm out of position with two opponents behind me, so bluffing here is thin and likely to be called or raised by heart holdings or decent pairs. Pot control is the priority — I want to preserve my 8.2k stack for better spots. I'll check and plan to fold to any meaningful bet, then reassess if we see a safe turn (or a free card) for a delayed bluff.

On a monotone board of all hearts, I have Ace-high with no heart in my hand. This means I have no flush draw and am vulnerable to any player holding even a single heart. Minni has checked, and since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Elon), the most prudent play is to check and see how the action develops. Betting here into two opponents without a piece of the board or a draw would be inefficient.

Preflop raise with Ah3d got three callers, building a nice pot. Flop 3h4hJh is a dream: paired the 3 for bottom pair with Ace kicker, and holding the nut flush draw (Ah with three hearts on board). Monster hand on a monotone draw-heavy board. Both opponents checked, indicating possible weakness or traps, but their histories show frequent calling stations. Betting for value against weaker pairs, ace-high, straight draws (52, 25, J5 etc.), or inferior draws. Protects against runner-runner straights or other flushes. Sizing 2/3 pot (600 into 900) balances value and fold equity while keeping pot manageable with deep stacks. Avoids check-raise setups or free cards to draws. Position allows control.

I completely missed this three‑heart flop with 9s6s and I’m out of position. Elon’s 600 into a 1500 pot requires ~28.6% equity to justify a call (600 to win 2100). My hand has virtually no showdown equity vs a reasonable c‑bet range (many heart combos, Jx, small pairs, and turn/river outs) — only distant runner‑runner spade possibilities. Raising as a bluff here is thin: Flash (BB) still acts behind me and Elon’s line is consistent with strong heart holdings or value that will not fold to a light raise. With a healthy 8.2k stack I should preserve chips for better spots. I will fold and wait for a more favorable situation.

I have Ace-high with no heart on a monotone heart board. Elon, the pre-flop aggressor, has followed up with a 2/3 pot bet. Without a heart, I have no flush draw, and my hand has very little equity to continue. If I call, I will be out of position with a weak hand on a dangerous board. Folding is the most disciplined play here to preserve my stack.

On the button with A♠2♣ against only the blinds, I should take the initiative and try to steal. A3x-A2x offsuit is a reasonable open for value/fold equity in position. I’ll raise to a standard 3x BB (300 total) to maximize fold equity vs the SB and pressure Elon in the BB while keeping the pot manageable. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll fold (A2o is a poor hand to get stacked with out of position); against a single call I’ll play aggressively in position on the flop.

I am holding King-Eight offsuit in the Small Blind. Minni opened from the Button with a 3x raise. While K8o has decent raw equity against a wide button opening range in a 3-handed game, playing it out of position against the chip stacks of Minni and Elon is not ideal. Calling often leads to difficult post-flop spots, and 3-betting as a bluff with this hand is unnecessary given my current chip lead. I will choose the disciplined route and fold, waiting for a more advantageous situation.

AhJh is an excellent hand heads-up against a button raise, which is likely wide given Minni's position and history of aggression from late position (e.g., Hands 10, 14, 25, 26). It has strong equity, dominates many Ax, KQ, suited broadways, and connectors in her range. We have position disadvantage OOP but great playability with flush and straight draws. Folding is -EV, calling is fine but allows her to realize equity easily and c-bet wide. 3-betting to 900 (3x her raise, standard sizing into 450 pot) for value/protection, builds pot with equity advantage, takes initiative, and pressures her lighter holdings. Effective stacks ~7900 deep enough to maneuver post-flop if called. Maximizes EV long-term.

I opened to 300 to steal from the button, but Elon’s 3‑bet to 900 is a sizable response. With A♠2♣ offsuit I’m often dominated by Ax and by pairs in a 3‑betting range; calling 600 into a 1,250 pot requires ~32% equity (600/(1250+600)) which A2o rarely has versus a standard 3‑bet range. A 4‑bet or shove would be too thin and unnecessarily high variance. I’ll preserve my ~7.9k stack and wait for a much clearer spot in this short‑handed game — fold.

I am on the button with 6-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest hands in the game. While I have a significant chip lead, there is no strategic value in playing this hand against Elon and Minni, both of whom have shown they are willing to defend their blinds or re-raise. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Preflop in SB with 3h5d offsuit, a speculative trash hand with poor playability and low equity against BB's wide defending range. Flash folded button, so heads-up vs Minni in BB. While SB steal opportunities exist, 35o is too weak to limp or raise profitably against a calling station like Minni who has shown frequent calls in history. Deep stacks (85BB), no need to risk chips with bottom-range hand; preserve stack for better spots in tournament.

Preflop on the button with 45o offsuit, a speculative hand with straight potential. SB likely folded (no action listed), BB limped, creating a good spot to raise for isolation, steal equity, or build a pot while in position. My stack is 85BB effective vs BB's deep stack, allowing aggression. History shows I've raised successfully from button with marginal hands. Standard raise size of 3x BB to 300 to take initiative and play postflop IP if called.

I'm in the small blind with 8♦7♥ facing a button open to 300 (pot 450). Calling the additional 250 (300 total committed) is a cheap price to see a flop with deep stacks — it gives ~35% immediate pot odds and good implied odds to hit straights/two-pairs. 3-betting this offsuit connector out of position is too ambitious and folding is too tight given the small investment. Plan: call and evaluate the flop; continue only with favorable connected/paired boards or strong draws, fold to heavy aggression on dry boards. If Flash 3-bets large pre-flop I'll fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 8h 4h. Elon raised to 300 from the Button, and Minni called from the Small Blind. Although I have good pot odds (calling 200 into a 700 pot), 84 offsuit is an extremely weak hand to play out of position against two opponents. With a significant stack lead in the tournament, there is no need to involve myself in marginal or losing situations. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I check. I have middle pair (7s) with an 8 kicker on an Ace-high flop — a decent but vulnerable hand out of position against the preflop raiser (Elon) who opens wide from the button. Betting OOP here bloats the pot and invites raises; checking controls the size and lets me evaluate Elon’s range. I will call a small-to-medium continuation bet (roughly up to ~½ pot) to extract value from worse pairs and floats and realise equity, but fold to heavy pot-sized bets or raises that strongly represent an Ace, two pair, or better. If Elon checks back I’m happy to take a cheap showdown or consider a small turn bet depending on the card.

Flop board Ac-7d-5h is somewhat coordinated with possible straight draws (46, 68) and backdoor flush draws, but dry enough for a continuation bet from my button range. I have bottom pair (5s) with a live 4 for a gutshot straight draw (4 outs to the 6), giving me decent showdown value and ~18-20% equity against most calling ranges. Minni checked from SB after calling my button raise preflop, which is often weak or speculative. C-betting ~60% pot takes initiative, denies equity to her draws/overs, protects my hand, and builds pot with my equity in position. Her history shows she calls multi streets but folds to aggression often (e.g., Hands 3,11,16). Sizing 450 balances value from Ax/pairs and bluffs from my range. Check-back would give free cards to her overcards/draws and miss value.

I have 8♦7♥ on 5♥7♦A♣ — second pair (sevens) with a decent 8-kicker, but it’s a vulnerable hand out of position. Elon opened wide from the button and fired a standard small-to-medium C-bet of 450 into the 1150 pot (~39% pot). That sizing gives me good immediate pot odds (calling 450 to win 1600 ≈ 28% required equity) and my hand has reasonable equity vs a wide c-betting range (A-highs, missed broadways, some bluffs, plus worse pairs). Raising OOP with a medium pair is too ambitious and folding is too nitty given the small cost and deep stacks. I will call to control the pot, realize equity, and re-evaluate on the turn — folding to a substantial raise or strong turn action (especially an Ace) and calling/controlling versus small turn bets or checks.

Elon checked the turn. I have second pair (7s) with an 8 kicker and I'm out of position; pot is 1,600 and stacks are deep (Minni 7,200; Elon 7,750). The 4♦ turn is a low card that doesn't complete any realistic draws — there is no possible five-card diamond flush with only one street left — and it only improves a small portion of Elon's range (A4, 44, 55). Betting OOP with a vulnerable medium pair here bloats the pot and exposes me to check-raises or value bets from better hands (Ax, sets). I will check to control the pot and realize equity; plan to call small river bets for pot odds but fold to significant river aggression that convincingly represents an Ace/two pair/set.

Turn 4d pairs my bottom card, giving me bottom two pair (fives and fours) on a board Ac-7d-5h-4d. This is a very strong hand, likely best against Minni's calling range from SB after limping pre and calling flop c-bet (probably mid pairs like 7x/6x, weak Ax, draws that missed). Board has diamond flush draw potential and possible 6 for straight, but my hand blocks some straights. She's checked again, showing weakness. Value bet 2/3 pot (~1100 into 1600) to extract from worse hands like 7x, 8x, Ax without 4/5, while protecting against draws. Position allows control, and history shows she calls stations but folds to big aggression sometimes - this size balances value/bluff. Checking risks giving free river card to draws or check-raise all-in (her stack deep). Pot committed trajectory favorable.

I have second pair (7s) with an 8-kicker, out of position on A‑5‑7‑4. Elon is the button who opened preflop and continued on the flop, then bet 1,100 into a 2,700 pot on the turn. Calling 1,100 to win a 3,800 pot requires ≈29% equity (1,100/3,800 ≈ 29%), which my hand has versus a wide BTN continuation range (many A‑x, missed broadways, bluffs, and some medium pairs). Folding is too nitty given the price; raising OOP with a vulnerable middle pair is too ambitious and will often put me in a worse spot against Ax or two pair. Plan: call the 1,100 to control the pot and realize equity, then re-evaluate on the river — fold to a substantial river raise or large sizing that strongly represents an Ace/two‑pair/set, call small river bets for pot odds, and go to showdown if he checks.

I have 8♦7♥ on 5♥-7♦-A♣-4♦-Q♣ — second pair (sevens) with a mediocre kicker and I’m out of position. Pot is 3,800 and it’s checked to me (min bet 100). Elon opened wide preflop and has been continuation-betting; his line is heavy with Ax, some two-pair/sets and occasional bluffs. Betting here has very little fold equity and will mostly get called by hands that beat me (Ax, two pair, sets) or fold out only the weakest spots — so a value bet is thin and a larger bluff is too risky given my stack and position. Checking and taking showdown preserves chips, lets me call a small river stab for good pot odds, and fold only to a strong raise that credibly represents a better hand. Preferred line: check to showdown.

River Qc adds a possible pair for hands like KQ, AQo, but her passive check-call lines preflop (limp-call raise), flop (check-call cbet), and turn (check-call large bet) suggest a capped range: weak Ax (AQ, AJ without much), mid pairs like 88-66/77 (beats me only if 77), busted draws (club/diamond/straight), or worse pairs like 55/44 (but board paired). Bottom two pair (5s over 4s) remains very strong here, ahead of most of her range (~70-80% equity vs calling range). History shows Minni frequently calls multi-street value (e.g., Hands 15,19,25,27,28,40) even into larger bets when behind. Checking behind misses value from her Ax/top pair and lets her realize equity for free. Value bet ~2/3 pot (2500 into 3800) extracts max value from worse, balanced size vs her stack (6100), denies draws/improves, and position protects vs unlikely jam. Pot-committed trajectory favors building stack now in tournament.

Board: 5♥ 7♦ A♣ 4♦ Q♣. I hold 8♦7♥ — second pair (sevens) with a mediocre kicker, out of position. Pot = 6,300; Elon bet 2,500; calling costs 2,500 to win 8,800 total, so required equity ≈ 28.4%.

Elon opened wide from the button, c-bet the Ace on the flop, barreled the turn and now fires a sizable river bet. That line is value-heavy (lots of A‑x, some two‑pair/sets, and Qx now that the Queen hit), but given his wide preflop range there will still be a nontrivial portion of missed hands/bluffs (missed broadways, Kx turn/river bluffs, thin floats) in his river-betting mix. My hand is behind much of his value range but beats pure air and many thin bluffs.

Raising here is a poor plan — I’m OOP with a vulnerable pair and a raise will get called by better hands and fold only the worst; folding is too tight given the price. The pot odds make a call reasonable, and I estimate my equity vs a realistic, value-weighted river-betting range is at or just above the ~28% threshold. Calling keeps the pot controlled, realizes my equity, and preserves stack flexibility for later hands if I’m wrong.

Action: call 2,500 to see showdown.

I’m on the button with A♣2♠ and 36bb. In a 3-handed pot this hand is worth playing aggressively — I have positional advantage versus both blinds and decent fold equity. Standard opening size at this table has been ~3x the big blind, so I’ll raise to 300 to try to steal the blinds or isolate one opponent and keep the pot manageable. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll strongly consider folding A2o (or re-evaluate based on sizing); if called I’ll use position to c-bet or control pot size postflop.

I am in the Small Blind with 7h 4h. Minni, who is currently the short stack with 33 BBs, has raised to 3x the big blind from the Button. While 74 suited has some playability, it is a very weak hand to defend out of position, especially with a 3x open. Given the stack sizes and the likelihood of Elon (the Big Blind) also getting involved or squeezing, the best play here is to fold and wait for a better spot, preserving my chip lead.

A3o is a standard 3-bet bluff from the BB facing a BTN open. It blocks strong Ax hands like AA, AK, AQ, has decent blocker equity and high-card strength if called. Fold equity is high against a wide button range. Postflop, ace blocker allows continuation as semi-bluff. Calling is loose OOP with poor kicker; folding misses value from button's wide range. Sizing to 900 (3x) is standard. Minni's short stack increases fold equity or allows easy fold to shove.

Elon’s 3‑bet to 900 after my button open to 300 is a strong line in a 3‑handed pot. A♣2♠ is easily dominated by Ax hands and has poor post‑flop playability versus a 3‑bet range. Calling 600 for a pot of ~1850 gives ~32% pot odds, but my equity against a realistic 3‑bet range is lower than that. 4‑betting or shoving would risk a big chunk of my ~33BB stack as a bluff against an opponent who can and will continue with better hands. Preserve stack and wait for a more favorable spot — fold.

I am on the button with Jc 4c. This is a speculative hand that plays reasonably well post-flop, especially in a short-handed environment. Minni, in the Big Blind, is very short-stacked with only 3200 chips (32 big blinds). A raise here would likely put significant pressure on her. However, given the pot odds and my position, a call (limping or completing) or a fold are also options. Given I have a massive stack advantage over both opponents and want to maintain pressure, but also need to be wary of Minni potentially shoving over a raise, I will raise to 300 to steal the blinds or see a flop in position.

Preflop in SB facing a button raise to 300 by Flash, with A5o offsuit. A5o is a marginal defending hand but playable against a likely wide button opening range (Flash has shown aggression in previous hands). Equity vs. ~40% button range is around 40-45%, plus dead money from blinds provides good implied odds. Out of position postflop, but ace blocker and straight potential justify a call rather than folding too tight. Raising is not ideal as A5o lacks strength for value 3-bet and is poor for bluffs OOP with short BB behind who might shove. Minni short-stacked at 32BB, but pot odds warrant calling 250 into 450 (1.8:1). Deep stacks allow maneuvering postflop if hit.

I’m on the big blind with Q♦2♣ facing a button raise to 300 and the small blind cold‑call (pot 700, I must call 200; min‑raise to 500). Q2o is a marginal, easily‑dominated hand and in a three‑way pot my postflop equity and playability are poor. Calling 200 into 700 gives ~22% pot odds, but realistic multiway equity versus a raiser+caller is typically lower — and when I pair my Q or 2 I’m often up against a better Q or a pair. I have no position and a healthy 32BB stack, so there’s no need to gamble or 3‑bet bluff (would be risky against Flash’s deep stack and an active caller). Fold and wait for a better, more playable spot.

Heads-up on the flop vs Flash on the button after calling his wide open raise preflop OOP with A5o. Flop is Qh9c7d, rainbow board with no hits to my hand: ace-high, no pair, no straight draw (gutshot to 6 is weak ~4% equity boost), no flush draw. My equity vs a typical button c-bet range (~50-60% hands continuing range) is around 25-30%, poor for OOP calling. Board favors my checking range (Ax, pairs below Q that missed). Flash aggressive historically (frequent button raises, c-bets), likely to c-bet wide for value/protection/fold equity. Betting OOP with air is -EV: blocks my own checking range, gets raised off equity, and doesn't get value. Check-fold most bets (unless tiny), check-call small ones if pot odds good and board favors villain overbetting bluffs, but prefer check-fold to preserve stack. Deep effective stacks (13k/23k), but no showdown value. Continuation postflop unnecessary with this texture.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and Elon has checked to me on a Queen-high board. While my hand (Jc 4c) is weak, this board texture favors the raiser's range. I will place a standard continuation bet of 350 (half the pot) to exert pressure and attempt to win the hand immediately. If called, I have some backdoor flush and straight possibilities.

Heads-up OOP vs Flash's button c-bet of 350 into 1050 (pot odds ~3:1) on Qh9c7d rainbow flop with As5h (ace-high, no pair, weak gutshot to 6 ~4% equity). Equity vs Flash's aggressive c-bet range (~50-60% including Qx, 98s, JTs, pairs, draws) is ~25-30%, insufficient for OOP call without fold equity or strong implied odds. Board connects well with villain's wide button opening range (Q,J,9,8 suited connectors common), while A5o has minimal showdown value and poor playability on later streets (reverse implied if villain has Q/A). Flash's history shows frequent button aggression (raises Hand 2,5,23,27,30,36 preflop; c-bets aggressively), likely betting wide for protection/value/fold equity. Calling risks stack commitment deep (13k effective) and commits to difficult turn decisions OOP with ~30% equity ceiling. Folding preserves stack for better spots in tournament (Hand 49/150, chip leader position). No blockers to villain's value hands; check-raise bluff too thin OOP vs aggro opponent.

3-handed on the button with 62dd suited connectors. Deep stacks overall (130BB effective vs Flash, 31BB vs Minni). Position is excellent for stealing blinds or playing post-flop. 62s is playable here due to suited connectivity, potential for straights/flushes, and strong implied odds. Opponents may defend wide, but position allows control. Standard button open size around 3x BB. Raising to 300 steals blinds frequently or builds pot OOP for them. Folding too tight; limping bad. Raise to apply pressure and maximize EV.

8♣3♠ is one of the worst starting hands — unsuited, unconnected, terrible high-card value. Elon opened to 300 from the button; I must call 250 more out of position and the big blind still acts behind. Calling 250 into a 450 pot requires ~35.7% equity just to break even right now, which 8-3o does not have versus a button opening range (and BB action will only make it worse). Elon has shown frequent aggression in previous hands so continuing out of position with this junk is likely to be costly. A small 3-bet bluff is possible in some spots, but with my hand, stack size (≈31.5BB) and opponents’ tendencies, folding and preserving chips for a better spot is correct.

I am holding Ace-Ten of Spades (As Ts) in the Big Blind. Elon opened from the Button to 300. This is a very strong hand to play against a button open. While calling is an option, raising (3-betting) is superior here to take the initiative, build a pot with a premium hand, and potentially end the hand pre-flop against Elon's wide button opening range. Given I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, a sizing of approximately 3x-3.5x his raise is standard. I will raise to 1,000.

Facing a 3-bet from Flash in the BB after my BTN raise with 62dd suited gappers. Position is still excellent post-flop. Hand has strong playability: suited for flush draws, connector potential for straights, good implied odds with deep effective stacks (~127BB). Pot odds excellent: calling 700 into 1350 (getting ~1.9:1 direct, plus implied). Equity ~35%+ vs polarized 3-bet range (value like 88+, AQ+, bluffs like Axs, suited connectors). Folding too tight for this spot; denies equity realization in position. 4-betting bluff possible but unnecessary risk vs deep-stacked Flash who may call wide or 5-bet. Calling maximizes EV by controlling pot OOP for opponent while leveraging position.

I was the pre-flop aggressor with a 3-bet to 1,000. The flop is Jack-high (Jh 9s 2h), which contains a few draws and hits some of Elon's potential calling range like JTs, QJs, or mid-pairs. I have Ace-high with a backdoor nut flush draw. In this spot, I will check to control the pot size. Checking allows me to see how Elon responds and potentially realize my equity or catch a card on the turn without bloating the pot out of position against a deep stack.

Flop hits me perfectly for top pair with 62dd on 9s-2h-Jh board. Weak kicker (6) but still ahead of most of Flash's checking range after his BB 3-bet and check. Board is drawy (hearts, possible JT/OESD), so betting protects equity and extracts value from Ax, worse pairs (77-88), floats, and weak draws. Position allows control; checking back risks giving free cards to improve draws or allowing bluffs. Deep effective stacks (~120BB) support multi-street play. Standard c-bet size ~60% pot (2050 pot -> ~1200) balances value and protection without overbetting weak top pair. Avoids check-raise traps while building pot OOP for opponent.

I 3-bet pre-flop with Ace-Ten of Spades, but the flop of Jack-Nine-Deuce with two hearts doesn't significantly help my hand beyond a backdoor flush draw and one overcard (the Ace). Elon's lead for 1,200 into the pot is a sizeable bet, suggesting he either has a Jack, a strong draw, or a mid-pair that he's protecting. Calling out of position with just Ace-high and weak backdoor equity is unprofitable against this sizing, especially since many of his hands (like AJ, KJ, QJ, or JT) have me dominated or in a very poor equity position. I will fold and preserve my stack.

Three-handed on the button with 8♣7♥ I should be opening a wide range. Raising takes the initiative, isolates the blinds, and uses my positional advantage — calling 100 would be too passive and lets the SB see a cheap flop out of position. My stack (~31 BB) supports a standard 3x open; I’ll raise to 300 total to get fold equity and keep the pot manageable. Plan: continue with aggression post‑flop when I hit or realize equity; fold to a large 3‑bet (big sizing) but consider calling smaller 3‑bets to play post‑flop in position.

In this 3-handed situation, I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Eight suited (Js 8s). Minni, the Button, has raised to 3x (300). With a stack of 22750, I have a massive lead, while Minni is short-stacked with only 2850 (less than 30 BB). Calling 250 more to see a flop is a standard play here; the hand has good playability and I am getting nearly 3:1 immediate pot odds. Raising against a short stack might force a shove that I'd rather not call with Jack-high, whereas calling allows me to evaluate the flop and potentially stack her if I hit well, given the low SPR (Stack-to-Pot Ratio).

K6o is a marginal hand OOP facing a button raise and SB flat call. Pot odds are 3.5:1 (call 200 into 700), but postflop play multiway OOP with this hand is difficult due to poor playability, reverse implied odds with bad kicker, and no suitedness or connectivity. Minni's button raise could be wide, but Flash's call tightens the range slightly. Folding preserves my deep stack (140BB) for better spots in this tournament stage. No need to speculate with a weak king.

On this flop of 8d-Td-Qc, I have bottom pair (8s) and a gutshot straight draw (9). I am first to act against the pre-flop aggressor, Minni. Given the low SPR (Stack-to-Pot Ratio) of approximately 4, and the fact that this board texture likely hits a button opening range quite well, checking is the most prudent action. Checking allows me to see if Minni continues with a bet. With bottom pair and a gutshot, I have enough equity to likely call a standard-sized continuation bet, but leading out myself doesn't accomplish much value-wise and could expose me to a raise or shove from her short stack.

I opened wide on the button and flopped a pair of eights (8♣7♥ on Q♣ T♦ 8♦). Flash (SB) checked to me. In position and having connected, I should continue to protect and extract thin value. The board has two diamonds (so some of his calling range can have a diamond draw) and there are hands (Qx/Tx, J9) that already beat me, so I don't want to inflate the pot too much. A half‑pot c-bet (~350 into 700) pressures missed broadways, charges draw-heavy hands, and gets value from weaker pairs and draws while keeping the pot manageable with a ~28.5BB stack. If he check‑raises big I'll fold or proceed very cautiously; if he calls I'll re‑evaluate on the turn.

On this flop of 8d-Td-Qc, I have bottom pair (8s) and a gutshot straight draw (9). Minni has made a continuation bet of 350 into a pot of 700, giving me 3:1 pot odds (calling 350 to win 1400 total). I need approximately 25% equity to make this call profitable. Between my pair of eights and the straight draw, I have enough potential to continue. Given Minni's short stack and the low SPR, I will call here to see if I improve on the turn, while also keeping her bluffs in the hand.

The turn 2d brings a potential flush and doesn't improve my hand, which remains bottom pair with a gutshot straight draw. Given that Minni was the pre-flop aggressor and continued on the flop, she may have a stronger hand or a flush draw that just completed. I will check to see how she reacts to the turn. If she checks behind, I get a free card to hit my gutshot. If she bets, I will re-evaluate based on the size of the bet relative to her remaining stack and the pot odds.

Turn completes a diamond-heavy texture (board Q♣ T♦ 8♦ 2♦). I have a marginal middle pair with no diamond (8♣7♥). I c-bet the flop and was called, but the 3rd diamond greatly increases the chance my opponent caught a flush or now has a hand that beats me (Qx/Tx/pocket pairs). With ~25BB effective and the pot already 1,400, I should avoid inflating the pot with a vulnerable hand. Checking controls the pot, keeps worse hands and bluffs in, and lets me reevaluate the river — I’ll call small river bets on safe boards but fold to large pressure or any clear made-flush action.

On the river, the board is 8d-Td-Qc-2d-Kc. I have a pair of Eights (8s Js). After both players checked the turn, the King of Clubs on the river is an interesting card. It completes some potential straights (if someone has a Nine) and puts a third high card on the board. Given that Minni checked the turn, she likely has a marginal made hand (like a Ten or a weak Queen) or a draw that missed. My pair of Eights has very little showdown value here, as almost any piece of the board or even a higher pocket pair beats me. However, checking is the most prudent action. I am out of position against a short stack; if I bet, I'm unlikely to fold out better hands given the pot odds she would receive, and if I check, I can potentially get to a cheap showdown or fold if she makes a significant bet.

I check. I only have a marginal pair (8♣7♥ = one pair of eights) on a final board Q♣ T♦ 8♦ 2♦ K♣. Villain called my flop c-bet and then played very passively (check–check on turn and check on river), which is consistent with top/mid pairs (Qx, Tx), slow-played pairs, or missed draws. A river bet would mostly get called by hands that beat me and fold the very few worse hands — so betting is negative EV. The pot is already 1,400 and I want to control risk with ~2,500 behind, so checking to showdown is the best line.
